item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this when I could take it as a one-and-done joke about pinkwashing. I liked it less when I could read it as a larger part of the GAW mythos, which unlike other storyline-driven SCP's like Anderson stuff, seems like it requires you to have already read all previous GAW articles.

I don't really care about the drama aspect of it for a variety of reasons, but I didn't really enjoy the overall effect of the article. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From reading the comments, I cannot agree with making "drama skips" at all. Those elements are undeniably a part of this, without needing to be in the article.

But the effect is interesting, so novote.

item.title: Re: new scp babies
item.content:encoded:
I love this, and it even gets to be my first upvote.
It's been ages since I checked up on the GAW stuff, and I love that Jude is both protecting his kids and also being the left wing terrorist shitposter I love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hell yeah my twitter account is an SCP now

Great little piece, I find GAW to be pretty all over the place in terms of actual political ideology, sometimes they seem like a really cool anomalous political activist group, sometimes they seem to just be a bunch of terrorist shitposters with no rhyme or reason, all depending on who is writing them, but I liked this one a lot. Short, sweet, to the point, provides some good bit of story on top of the skip itself, and the tale is very engaging and fun too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure what you're saying here– the SCP isn't causing brands to post about Pride or LGBT activism, and it isn't making those initiatives backfire: it's going in and anomalously changing the posts that brands already make about those topics into something more honest.

There's nothing in the article that 'magically explains' anything that actually happens in our world. The effects of SCP-4493 aren't a real thing that happens in our world (unfortunately).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article fails for the very same reason a terrible scpfication of krokodil failed many years ago - it tosses in a magical explanation for something (backfiring and miscarried social initiatives) that is fundamentally less interesting than the subject itself.

However, at least it's better written than said SCPfication of Krokodil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this rules i wish it was real

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, given it's topical, and also sheds a light on the very real exploitation of Pride by companies who mostly do it to make a profit.

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: damn. if only you could click options and use the edit function

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Twitter should be an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hey

rude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how to feel about this one. I really like the concept, but i don't really understand what is so dangerous about this that O5 would get involved. It feels like its missing something, like more incident reports or something to exaggerate the seriousness of this anomaly. As of now it just feels a bit too stale.

Maybe part of it is my unfamiliarity with GAW and maybe its because i didn't read the sister tale, but i don't feel like SCPs should have to rely on that kind of thing either.

Abstaining from a vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely feel some kind of Way about this piece. I will get back to you when I figure out what that Way is, exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is written very well, but IMHO I don't get what people like about gaw. Anyways +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Won't lie, I was a bit worried when I first started reading that it would be just be an excuse for LGBT-bashing under the guise of fiction.

Glad I was wrong. At the same time, I'm glad it wasn't overly in support to the detriment of the narrative.

The log with the O5 was great too.

+1

item.title: Re: new scp babies
item.content:encoded: wow thanks officer nebula

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the core premise here, this is really well-done.

item.title: it's back to +69 so...
item.content:encoded:

⁂gunch


ncie

  • HUMS 6.9m

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean plane?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was a wonderful read. Take my upboat! 🌈

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not really enjoy this article nor the tale that accompanied it.
Edit: I have reread the tale. I suppose it is kinda funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the article, both the general message and especially the interview.

To elaborate on what Hippo already said, I find that the idea of the Foundation diplomatically dealing with the GAW to be really interesting and a very welcome view. It makes the Foundation look more competent and realistic, showing that they are capable of being flexible and approaching different things in different ways, instead of just taking a single-minded approach to all groups and anomalies and causing more problems than there were to begin with. The GAW is already pretty chill, so going the diplomatic route is less risky than directly antagonizing them, which could make them a bigger threat than they currently are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't get a chance to look at this as a draft (as my life has recently been — sometimes quite literally — on fire); that being said, there's a lot of elements I like (and a few I don't). Let's start with the latter:

CRITICISMS
– hide block

My main criticisms are a lack of tightness regarding the prose and a few missed opportunities. For example: Having the posts 'undeletable' is the digital equivalent of objects that are 'indestructible'. It's a clear "signal" that the author just needs it that way to facilitate the story (which is fine, but I feel like there's a more creative way to do this).

The description could be shorter (like SCP-2726, the anomaly isn't terribly important; it's the ethical implications and the interview that matters), and the anomaly itself feels "at odds" with how it works in the tale. We're told that it modifies posts to reflect a negative impact — but in the tale, it sounds more like someone's editing these posts themselves. I guess the anomaly could be sapient? But that's never implied. We're left to believe these edits are just objectively true, and reflective of reality — despite the posts in the tale having a clear "not-so-objective" personality.

Now, for the rest:

POSITIVES
– hide block

The opening line of the containment procedure is an excellent hook. The core concept of the anomaly itself (something that exposes the hypocrisy of "performative wokeness") is a clever way to sidestep the problem of the article itself being performative (so clever I'm mildly annoyed I didn't think of it myself). And the interview… I really like the interview.

There's elements I don't like in the interview, but they all boil down to tiny nitpicks (I dislike the specifity of "twenty two seconds", I feel like some of the formating could be cleaner, etc). The dialogue was smooth, and I really liked the revelation that the Foundation is in cahoots with Jude — and the second revelation that the Foundation is, for whatever reason, afraid of him. This might not sync with everyone, but it's an idea I find interesting (and subversive).

All in all, this was an interesting article, and I enjoyed it enough to upvote by the end. I think part of me kind of wishes a part of the tale (Rainbow-Crapitalism) was included in the body of the article itself, as I feel like the tale and article work so well together that they might as well be the same document (plus, the comedy of the tale could cause some interesting dissonance with the more serious interview at the end), but that's a lukewarm criticism at best.

+1!

item.title: Re: OPERATIONAL STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
Here's a little tip: you're not supposed to respond. That's what the "closed" part means. Take it up in PMs if you really need be.

This is a closed staff post. Do not reply if you aren't staff.

item.title: Re: OPERATIONAL STAFF POST - CLOSED

This is a closed staff post. Do not reply if you aren't staff.

item.title: OPERATIONAL STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
Hey, funny thing: your comment is pretty clearly a thinly-veiled attack on more than just the piece. That might fly on RPC, but it doesn't here. I'm gonna ask you not to do that again.

This is a closed staff post. Do not reply if you aren't staff.

item.title: Re: new scp babies
item.content:encoded: Another great SCP Kinch! You’re such a good writer, love seeing your work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP 4493 represents everything wrong with the SCP Foundation. It is an unnecessarily insipidly droll piece of political commentary that fails to hook the readers in a truly engaging piece when the piece does nothing more than exploit a movement and its people in the same exact fashion as corporations do. Only, instead of exploiting it for monetary value, the author's exploitation of the month serves to guarantee upvotes based solely upon the political notions it revolves around.

The piece fails to establish any scientifically charged descriptions and arguments in regards to this phenomenon, which isn't even a phenomenon to begin with. Can't exactly call it an anomaly if it doesn't exhibit any sort of unusual or anomalous properties that would justify this relatively unnecessary keter class entity. Let us not forget that it takes potshots at users whom the author hasn't even been subtle about, I.E. N. Krum, makes this piece comes across as childish and ill-mannered. Though it is truly a hilarious piece, but for all the wrong reasons, it is only by how painfully obvious this was fueled not by passion or the strive to write a good piece, but anger and a misguided sense of justice that comes across as unrealistic and unreasonable.

It lacks any kind of imaginative or intuitive creative instances that sets it apart from reality. I dare say it's just reality albeit skewered in a way that it tries to invoke emotion rather than critical thinking. The tone is completely off the mark and for every criticism for each new or old user desperately vying to follow the old formula that's become stagnant and restrictive that to have the administration condone 4493's existence says a lot about what their priorities are and where they place them at. It is uninspiring, hypocritical, exploitative and mean spirited.

-1

item.title: Re: new scp babies
item.content:encoded: Don't know what about this post could be "laughing my ass off" worthy, but something tells me your downvote indicates that this post is not made entirely in good faith, nor would it adhere to the Criticism Policy were you to expand and elaborate on your answer.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
So first of all, JimmyBoyHaha, do not backseat moderate.

Second of all, I am closing this line of discussion. Do not reply to this post.

I've asked some RPC people about it. Apparently, it's directed to a different user, the 2721 number being… A "meme number." Can't speak for them, but I hope it helps.

item.title: (no title)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is not victim blaming, not by a mile. My intention was to prevent a bigger sandstorm, and not to "victim blame."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. I won't say something among the lines of "Give the other cheek then," but this shouldn't be turned into a chicken game. Just a suggestion.

For the record, I feel it's justified. Not sure why Blairin is also mentioned, but I assume something similar happened with him.

item.content:encoded: yea lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Under no circumstances is the SCP Foundation, any of its subsidiary companies, or any individual employee thereof to profess pride in or support of an LGBT identity during the month of June.

Hah. Love this article. +1

item.title: Re: new scp babies
item.content:encoded: lmao

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyable read, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, at one point they had a whole article making fun of and defaming her. A little reference to a pair of names is nothing compared to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: telling someone they're inviting harassment for "provoking" a nazi who sent her death threats feels an awful lot like victim blaming :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't start the fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a nice little article. I absolutely love the concept, reminds me of an episode of Fairy Oddparents I watched a billion years ago.

Somewhat refreshing, but I would've liked it more if it had more screencaps… They make for nice memes, to be honest. The interview is alright but I think it distracts from the best part of the article. I'm yet to read the tale.

However…

Three police officers (Sgt. N. Krum, Officer B. Smith, and Officer L. Aaron) are highlighted to have used their positions to carry out numerous crimes on individuals within the city. The three police officers were later found dead in their respective homes. All are said to have died of heart attacks with no sign of foul play.

Sgt. N. Krum. Those three words fit a number of definitions, a few of them being bruh moment, completely unasked for, and why though. The implications of the officers being dead from heart attacks seems to speak for the writers' very soul and I don't really like that.
Otherwise, I think it's a dangerous provocation that could lead to a repetition of last June, should the climate be slightly less stable. I cannot comprehend how did that reference pass writing process. Like, sure, go give Enkrum more publicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will forever regret mispelling “seems”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I commented on the story, but I'm commenting here to say that this is still really cool, at least where it comes to 'meting out justice like you get to decide what other people get to know about who they're consuming from' and 'pulling back the curtain behind corporations with massive human rights violations' (i.e. almost all of them, probably). It's…unfortunate that the Foundation wanted to try and stop it, but it WAS anomalous, technically speaking! Mind you, so are many of Marshall, Carter and Dark's dealings, but if I remember right the Foundation raids them anyway.

Something I feel like you could emphasise a *little* bit more is the fridge horror with the potential information breach - at least ONE of the SCPs will be LGBT+ in some way (like the one who exudes that drug that makes people slaves to him, and is very apologetic about it)! What happens when the Foundation staff's twitter accounts start posting pride month stuff? What happens when the government does it?? Not that they've done, or will do, that anytime soon thanks to Trump's government, though. This could go really bad, AND none of the employees can celebrate pride, which I'm going to measure as about the same level of awful.

This is really interesting, though, and it's a shame it's so short, I feel - it could do with a bit more expounding upon, even if I'm not sure how. Presumably it has a horrific effect on the economy for example, or corporations eventually stop posting about pride altogether - thus limiting visibility!

It's really sad how even this solution has unmitigated potential negative effects, because it's the system people live under that's the problem, not the individual actions. Whether or not you include it is obvs up to you, but it feels as though the negative effects of it, or events in general, could be emphasised here. At the very least, it's a fucking bureaucratic/administrative nightmare, right?

Anyway, +1 from me :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The science isn’t in yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We just dont know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What ever do you mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have expected something degrading. "Sergeant" seams almost like a compliment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the whole "hey someone from the O5 is somewhat sanctioning these folks and really doesn't feel like enforcing the actual agreement" bit here detracts way too much for me. I don't know what it is about it other than just the feeling that the Foundation (and Six in specific) seems to be way way waaaaaaayyy too lenient for reasons that don't quite gel. The idea that it would, essentially, be too much of a pain to try and combat GAW feels sketchy at best, given the lengths that the Foundation tends to go to in other cases (as a general concept, not in a specific "the Foundation has to act this way cuz canon"). And then after their figurehead here breaks the agreement a member of the Council just says "eh whatever" without actual repercussions feels like we're giving a lot of leeway for no reason.

Good to see whomever is naming ops thinks Hackers was dope, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GAW left a terrible first impression (looking at you, 2433) , but it's kind of grown on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
😘
this is real good love it
particularly like the "Not at all. Not even a little bit", that shit was phenomenal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He’s not paling around so much as very politically trying to keep his kids safe from the Foundation while also promising the foundation he’s going to make sure they’re doing nothing violent (which is like what he was going to do anyway). I think it’s very much a deal with the devil he comes to regret. And as we see in 4669, one he directly goes against. I want this article to hint at where Jude’s going. He’s realized he’ll never be able to see eye to eye with the foundation. In the ten days after the interview, one may even guess 4669 was born.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
first thought: i'm not hypercomfortable with the idea that jude's palling around with O5s and shit but then, i guess that might just be how some of the gawers would feel as well. on the other hand, I love that the Foundation ""can't celebrate Pride anymore"". poor them >:((( if only they could >:(((

The line "I put the little bit of me in that shout about the new Mister Against Weed" is a little hard to follow at first, and could maybe use rephrasing if you want what Jude means to come across more plainly. I care about this because I do love the twist-thesis that, hey, all of the evil corporate groups-of-interest are in bed with the evil corporate groups-of-non-interest.

I think the Ms. Zap bit is cool, but it kind of intersects this article without adding to it very much. It's like she waved at me and I waved back, which was cool but not, like, as relevant as I want it to be.

also: lamda-69, nice
